for sponsorship reasons, is a [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") in the suburb of [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Queensland "Milton, Queensland"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia. Nicknamed **the Cauldron**, it is a three-tiered rectangular sporting stadium with a capacity of 52,500.[[6]](#cite_note-capacity-6) The traditional home of [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Brisbane, the modern stadium is also now used for [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") and [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and has a rectangular [playing field](/wiki/Pitch_(sports_field) "Pitch (sports field)") of 136 by 82 metres (446 by 269 ft). The stadium's major tenants are the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), the [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)"), the [Brisbane Roar](/wiki/Brisbane_Roar_FC "Brisbane Roar FC"), the [Queensland Maroons](/wiki/Queensland_rugby_league_team "Queensland rugby league team") and the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds").

**Brisbane Stadium (Lang Park)**,[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) currently known as **Suncorp Stadium** for sponsorship reasons, is a [multi-purpose stadium](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") in the suburb of [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Queensland "Milton, Queensland"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), Australia. Nicknamed **the Cauldron**, it is a three-tiered rectangular sporting stadium with a capacity of 52,500.[[6]](#cite_note-capacity-6) The traditional home of [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Brisbane, the modern stadium is also now used for [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") and [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and has a rectangular [playing field](/wiki/Pitch_(sports_field) "Pitch (sports field)") of 136 by 82 metres (446 by 269 ft). The stadium's major tenants are the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), the [Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)"), the [Brisbane Roar](/wiki/Brisbane_Roar_FC "Brisbane Roar FC"), the [Queensland Maroons](/wiki/Queensland_rugby_league_team "Queensland rugby league team") and the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds").

Lang Park was established in 1914, on the site of the former [North Brisbane Cemetery](/wiki/North_Brisbane_Burial_Ground "North Brisbane Burial Ground"); in its early days it was home to a number of different sports, including cycling, athletics and soccer. The lease of the park was taken over by the [Brisbane Rugby League](/wiki/Brisbane_Rugby_League_premiership "Brisbane Rugby League premiership") in 1957, before it became the home of the game in Queensland (remaining so to this day). It has also been the home ground of major rugby union and soccer matches in Queensland since its modern redevelopment, including the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") and the [Brisbane Roar](/wiki/Brisbane_Roar_FC "Brisbane Roar FC"), as well as some [Wallabies](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team"), [Matildas](/wiki/Australia_women%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia women's national soccer team") and [Socceroos](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") matches. It hosted the [2008](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup_final "2008 Rugby League World Cup final") and [2017 Rugby League World Cup finals](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup_final "2017 Rugby League World Cup final"), besides the [2021 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2021_NRL_Grand_Final "2021 NRL Grand Final"). In addition to this, the ground hosted [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup") quarter finals and two [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") grand finals, with the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") winning on both occasions.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The venue hosted several matches for the [2023 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2023_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2023 FIFA Women's World Cup") including the third-place match, and will host the rugby sevens and soccer tournaments at the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics"), including the gold medal matches in both the men's and women's soccer events.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The site of Lang Park was originally the [North Brisbane Cemetery](/wiki/North_Brisbane_Burial_Ground "North Brisbane Burial Ground"), and until 1875 was Brisbane's primary [cemetery](/wiki/Cemetery "Cemetery"). By 1911, the area was heavily populated, so the Paddington Cemeteries Act (1911) was introduced, and the site was redeveloped as a recreational site. In 1914, it was fenced off and named Lang Park after [John Dunmore Lang](/wiki/John_Dunmore_Lang "John Dunmore Lang").[[13]](#cite_note-ref-officialsite-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/StateLibQld_1_194039_Shot_for_goal_during_a_soccer_match_in_Brisbane%2C_ca._1937.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_194039_Shot_for_goal_during_a_soccer_match_in_Brisbane%2C_ca._1937.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_194039_Shot_for_goal_during_a_soccer_match_in_Brisbane,_ca._1937.jpg)

1937 soccer match at Lang Park [Milton](/wiki/Milton,_Queensland "Milton, Queensland") (looking towards Milton Road) – teams not known

The ground was leased by the Queensland Amateur Athletics Association (QAAA) in the 1920s. In 1935, the [Queensland Soccer Council](/wiki/History_of_association_football_in_Brisbane,_Queensland "History of association football in Brisbane, Queensland") (QSC) became a sub-tenant of the QAAA, with a view to using it as the home ground for Brisbane soccer fixtures (leaving its former home, the [Brisbane Cricket Ground](/wiki/The_Gabba "The Gabba")).[[15]](#cite_note-15) The [Latrobe Soccer Club](/wiki/Bardon_Latrobe_FC "Bardon Latrobe FC"), in turn, became a sub-tenant of the QSC, using the ground for its home games (see image below).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

However, by 1937, the QSC was considering sub-leasing Lang Park to "another code of football" (most likely Western Suburbs Rugby League) as it "was not satisfied with the financial returns ... under the sub-lease to the Latrobe-Milton club".[[16]](#cite_note-16) Latrobe in turn responded that "'If no action Is taken to introduce the Ipswich clubs into the Brisbane competition this' season ... the Latrobe-Milton Club cannot accept an increase in rental for Lang Park. Give us competition play with Ipswich and my club will hold the ground as headquarters for the code."[[17]](#cite_note-17)

On 11 February 1950, the official opening of the Lang Park Police Citizens Youth Club took place and youth activities commenced because of the concerns with the increase of juvenile delinquency. Activities such as boxing, wrestling, basketball and gymnastics all occur at these premises to this day. Contemporaneous records are scant, but it appears the QSC did not renew the lease the ground after the intervening World War II. In 1953 the [Brisbane Rugby League](/wiki/Brisbane_Rugby_League_premiership "Brisbane Rugby League premiership") (BRL) amalgamated with the [Queensland Rugby League](/wiki/Queensland_Rugby_League "Queensland Rugby League") (QRL). QRL secretary [Ron McAullife](/wiki/Ron_McAuliffe "Ron McAuliffe") negotiated a 21-year lease of Lang Park from the [Brisbane City Council](/wiki/Brisbane_City_Council "Brisbane City Council") in order to give the QRL a financially viable base of operations. The park had only the most basic facilities, and the QRL contributed £17,000 to its development. Lang Park hosted its first game of first grade rugby league during the 1930s, with regular BRL games commencing there in 1955. In [1958](/wiki/1958_Brisbane_Rugby_League_season "1958 Brisbane Rugby League season"), it hosted its first [Brisbane rugby league grand final](/wiki/Brisbane_Rugby_League_premiership#Grand_Final_results "Brisbane Rugby League premiership") in which [Brothers](/wiki/Past_Brothers "Past Brothers") defeated [Valleys](/wiki/Fortitude_Valley_Diehards "Fortitude Valley Diehards") 22 points to 7.[[18]](#cite_note-Gallaway-18) A record crowd of 19,824 saw Northern Suburbs defeat Fortitude Valley at Lang Park in the BRL grand final in September 1961.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the 1960s, [Fonda Metassa](/wiki/Fonda_Metassa "Fonda Metassa") famously burst from the back of an ambulance to return to the field after being carted off injured in a match for Norths against Redcliffe. As the ground was used increasingly by the QRL, it became no longer viable for use as a public recreation facility due to spoilage of the running track.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 1962, the Lang Park Trust was created under an act of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Queensland "Parliament of Queensland"). This allowed for the construction of the Frank Burke Stand (1962), [Ron McAuliffe](/wiki/Ron_McAuliffe "Ron McAuliffe") Stand (1975), and the Western Grandstand (1994). The Trust had on its board one member from the [Queensland Government](/wiki/Queensland_Government "Queensland Government"), one member from the [Brisbane City Council](/wiki/Brisbane_City_Council "Brisbane City Council"), two members from the [Queensland Rugby League](/wiki/Queensland_Rugby_League "Queensland Rugby League") and one member from the [Brisbane Rugby League](/wiki/Brisbane_Rugby_League_premiership "Brisbane Rugby League premiership").[[21]](#cite_note-McGregor-21)

From the 1960s, Lang Park hosted interstate and international rugby league, including the [inaugural State of Origin match](/wiki/1980_State_of_Origin_game "1980 State of Origin game"). Up until 1972, it was the home ground of the [Western Suburbs Panthers](/wiki/Wests_Panthers "Wests Panthers").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### NSWRL/ARL years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NSWRL/ARL years")]

In 1988, the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") entered the [NSWRL premiership](/wiki/NSWRL_premiership "NSWRL premiership") along with the Gold Coast Chargers and the Newcastle Knights. The Broncos played out of Lang Park from 1988 until 1992, when they moved to the 60,000 capacity [ANZ Stadium](/wiki/Queensland_Sport_and_Athletics_Centre "Queensland Sport and Athletics Centre"), the stadium for the [1982 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games"). The move occurred due to a dispute over the Broncos sponsor, [Power's Brewery](/wiki/Power%27s_Brewery "Power's Brewery"), being a competitor of the QRL's sponsor [XXXX](/wiki/XXXX_(beer) "XXXX (beer)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1994, the Land Park stadium's name was changed to Suncorp Stadium, when naming sponsorship was attained by Queensland financial institution, [Suncorp](/wiki/Suncorp_Group "Suncorp Group"). Also in 1994, the Western Stand was built, replacing the Frank Burke Stand.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On 25 May 1997, the 1996/1997 [National Soccer League](/wiki/National_Soccer_League "National Soccer League") Grand final was played in front of then a capacity crowd of 40,446, where the [Brisbane Strikers FC](/wiki/Brisbane_Strikers_FC "Brisbane Strikers FC") defeated [Sydney United FC](/wiki/Sydney_United_58_FC "Sydney United 58 FC") 2–0.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Redevelopment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Suncorp_Stadium%2C_Brisbane_seen_from_the_top_of_135_Coronation_Drive%2C_in_March_2019.jpg/250px-Suncorp_Stadium%2C_Brisbane_seen_from_the_top_of_135_Coronation_Drive%2C_in_March_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suncorp_Stadium,_Brisbane_seen_from_the_top_of_135_Coronation_Drive,_in_March_2019.jpg)

The stadium in March 2019

Lang Park was heavily redeveloped in the early 2000s into a 52,500 all-seater state of the art rectangular stadium.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Suncorpstadium071006.JPG/250px-Suncorpstadium071006.JPG)](/wiki/File:Suncorpstadium071006.JPG)

Suncorp Stadium before the [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") vs [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team") [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") international in October 2006.

Lang Park suffered significant damage during the [2010–2011 Queensland floods](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Queensland_floods "2010–2011 Queensland floods") with the entire playing field being covered by flood water.[[24]](#cite_note-24) An electrical fire started in a transformer room due to water ingress, however there was no major damage from the fire.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Today

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Today")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_01.jpg/250px-Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_01.jpg)

View of playing field from southern end, 13 July 2022.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_03.jpg/250px-Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suncorp_Stadium_Brisbane_Australia_03.jpg)

Suncorp Stadium southern entrance, 13 July 2022

Although the stadium has been the traditional home of [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in Queensland, it has also become the state's premier venue for [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), as well as [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"). The re-developed stadium first hosted rugby union games at the [2003 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup") and in 2005, the stadium became the new home of the [Queensland Reds](/wiki/Queensland_Reds "Queensland Reds") [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") team when they moved from their former home at [Ballymore Stadium](/wiki/Ballymore_Stadium "Ballymore Stadium").[[1]](http://townsvillebulletin.news.com.au/common/story_page/0,7034,16773958%255E23217,00.html) This move caused some disquiet amongst rugby traditionalists, however was accepted by Queensland Rugby Union CEO Theo Psaros, who said that "our hearts may be at Ballymore but our heads say it's time to move."[[26]](#cite_note-26) The year before the Reds' move, the newly established football team [Queensland Roar](/wiki/Brisbane_Roar_FC "Brisbane Roar FC") of the [A-League](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men") also elected to play their home games at Suncorp Stadium.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[New Zealand rugby journalist](/wiki/The_New_Zealand_Herald "The New Zealand Herald") Wynne Gray called Suncorp Stadium perhaps the best rugby stadium in the world. "It is so intimate you can hear the smack of bodies, the boot on leather, you feel the power and rhythm of the games."[[27]](#cite_note-ref-gray-27)

The stadium has also been favourably compared to Cardiff's [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") and London's [Twickenham Stadium](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 29 July 2006, the [Bledisloe Cup](/wiki/Bledisloe_Cup "Bledisloe Cup") clash between the [Wallabies](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_union_team "Australia national rugby union team") and the [All Blacks](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") returned to Brisbane for the first time in over a decade for the [2006 Tri Nations Series](/wiki/2006_Tri_Nations_Series "2006 Tri Nations Series"). Though Australia narrowly lost the match, the game saw a new ground record set.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Suncorp_Stadium.jpg/250px-Suncorp_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suncorp_Stadium.jpg)

Inside Suncorp Stadium during an [NRL](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") game

A month later on 7 October the stadium hosted a 1–1 friendly soccer game between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team") in which [Tony Vidmar](/wiki/Tony_Vidmar "Tony Vidmar"), [Stan Lazaridis](/wiki/Stan_Lazaridis "Stan Lazaridis"), [Zeljko Kalac](/wiki/Zeljko_Kalac "Zeljko Kalac") and goal scorer [Tony Popovic](/wiki/Tony_Popovic "Tony Popovic") all retired from international soccer.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 8 November 2006, a crowd of 44,358 saw the [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") play against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team") for the last time.

On Wednesday 13 and Thursday 14 December, the stadium hosted its first music concert since the 1980s and the stadium's redevelopment when [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams") performed in front of two 52,413 sell-out crowds during his "Close Encounters" tour of Australia, and was the venue for the [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") 360 tour in December 2010. That same month the stadium hosted [Bon Jovi](/wiki/Bon_Jovi "Bon Jovi") as part of [The Circle Tour](/wiki/The_Circle_Tour "The Circle Tour").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Stadium was also the site of the [2011 A-League Grand Final](/wiki/2011_A-League_Grand_Final "2011 A-League Grand Final"), drawing a crowd of over 50,000. The match was one of the most dramatic in A-League history, with the [Brisbane Roar](/wiki/Brisbane_Roar_FC "Brisbane Roar FC") scoring two goals in the last five minutes to level the scores with the [Central Coast Mariners](/wiki/Central_Coast_Mariners_FC "Central Coast Mariners FC") after several hundred home supporters had left the stadium early, many returning after hearing the stadium erupt while waiting for the train. The Roar went on to win 4–2 in the penalty shootout, making for an incredible victory.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The stadium is also home to the Lang Park Police Citizens Youth Club.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

American Singer-Songwriter [Taylor Swift](/wiki/Taylor_Swift "Taylor Swift") played at Suncorp Stadium for her [Red Tour](/wiki/The_Red_Tour "The Red Tour") on 7 December 2013.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In September 2016, it was announced that the video screens, originally installed in 2003, would be replaced.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Construction started on the new video screens in March 2017 and was finished in early May 2017 in time for an NRL double header.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2019, the stadium hosted the NRL's inaugural [Magic Round](/wiki/Magic_Round_(NRL) "Magic Round (NRL)"), in which all eight matches in a single round are played at the one venue.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The NRL has an agreement to host its Magic Round in Brisbane until the 2027 season.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The 2025 Magic Round was held at Suncorp Stadium from 1 to 4 May 2025 with a total attendance of 149,329.

In 2020, the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") played their "home" finals at the venue, as it was not possible for the team to play them at its regular home ground, [AAMI Park](/wiki/Melbourne_Rectangular_Stadium "Melbourne Rectangular Stadium"), due to the state of Victoria being locked down during the state's second wave of [coronavirus](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") infections.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

On 26 June 2021, the [Queensland Maroons](/wiki/Queensland_rugby_league_team "Queensland rugby league team") played at home against the [New South Wales Blues](/wiki/New_South_Wales_rugby_league_team "New South Wales rugby league team") in the [State of Origin series](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series"). Queensland lost the game 26–0, and henceforth the series.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Due to a [COVID-19 lockdown in New South Wales](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_South_Wales#Bondi_cluster "COVID-19 pandemic in New South Wales"), which began on 26 June 2021 and was still in effect into October, the stadium hosted twenty-one extra games on top of its normal commitments to the Brisbane Broncos as well as Magic Round, including the [2021 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2021_NRL_Grand_Final "2021 NRL Grand Final") on 3 October 2021.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) This was the second time that a rugby league premiership Grand Final was played outside of Sydney, following the [1997 Super League Grand Final](/wiki/1997_Super_League_(Australia)_season#Grand_final "1997 Super League (Australia) season").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In October 2025, the [Premier of Queensland](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland"), [David Crisafulli](/wiki/David_Crisafulli "David Crisafulli"), announced that the stadium would receive a "next generation" renovation in time for the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics"). The stadium is expected to receive accessibility improvements, and an expansion in capacity.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Statue_of_Wally_Lewis_at_Lang_Park%2C_Milton%2C_Brisbane_02.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Wally_Lewis_at_Lang_Park%2C_Milton%2C_Brisbane_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Wally_Lewis_at_Lang_Park,_Milton,_Brisbane_02.jpg)

Wally Lewis statue outside Suncorp Stadium (Lang Park), Brisbane (2022)

In the 1980s, Brisbane [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") icon [Wally Lewis](/wiki/Wally_Lewis "Wally Lewis") became known as *The Emperor of Lang Park* after his performances in [State of Origin](/wiki/State_of_Origin_series "State of Origin series") matches played at the ground. Brisbane-based beer [XXXX](/wiki/XXXX_(beer) "XXXX (beer)"), which is brewed at the nearby [Castlemaine Brewery](/wiki/Castlemaine_Perkins "Castlemaine Perkins"), ran a television advertisement celebrating this title in song:

> :   Here's to Wally Lewis for lacing on a boot
> :   Sometimes he plays it rugged, sometimes he plays it cute
> :   He slices through a backline like a [Stradbroke Island](/wiki/Stradbroke_Island "Stradbroke Island") shark
> :   There's glue on all his fingers, he's the Emperor of Lang Park

— Castlemaine Perkins XXXX advertisement[[21]](#cite_note-McGregor-21)

In 2006, Queensland Minister for Sport, [Tom Barton](/wiki/Tom_Barton_(politician) "Tom Barton (politician)") introduced the Stadium's Sports Media Hall of Fame which honours the achievements of media representatives who have covered the major football codes played at this historic ground over the past 40 years.[[39]](#cite_note-39) So far, there are five inductees: rugby league commentator [George Lovejoy](/wiki/George_Lovejoy "George Lovejoy"), rugby league journalists [Jack Reardon](/wiki/Jack_Reardon_(rugby_league) "Jack Reardon (rugby league)") and Steve Ricketts, Gerry Collins and Frank O'Callaghan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Accessibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Accessibility")]

Controversially, the redevelopment was the first major sporting facility in Australia with no car parking, primarily due to concerns with traffic congestion in the surrounding residential neighbourhood. Instead, the stadium's is surrounded by pubs, restaurants, cafes, bars and the XXXX brewery. This together with dedicated pedestrian links to Milton railway station and Brisbane CBD adds to the match day experience and is seen as a model for new stadiums and large entertainment venues. The stadium redevelopment has been the catalyst for the Barracks urban renewal development at Petrie Terrace midway along the dedicated pedestrian link to the CBD.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

It also hosted three non Australia matches. Incidentally, they were all [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_league_team "England national rugby league team") matches. The first was a 1975 Rugby League World Cup match against [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") on 10 June 1975 with 6,000 in attendance and lost 12 – 7. The second was a 2008 Rugby League World Cup match against New Zealand on 15 November 2008 with 26,659 in attendance and lost 32 – 22. The third and final to date was a 2014 Four Nations match between against [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_league_team "Samoa national rugby league team") with 47,813 in attendance and was a double header which was followed by the Australia New Zealand match. England won 32 – 26.

Suncorp Stadium hosted two matches of the [2017 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup"). The venue played host to the first semi-final on 24 November and the tournament final on 2 December.

### Boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Boxing")]

Suncorp Stadium was the host of the [Manny Pacquiao vs. Jeff Horn](/wiki/Manny_Pacquiao_vs._Jeff_Horn "Manny Pacquiao vs. Jeff Horn") fight for the [WBO](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") [welterweight](/wiki/Welterweight "Welterweight") championship with 51,052 people in attendance.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lang_Park&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Controversies")]

On Thursday, 16 June 2011, The [Weekend Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian") revealed that Suncorp Stadium was in danger of either losing the hosting rights to all Queensland based NRL finals matches to Sydney, or having its capacity limited to 25,000 seats, due to a condition included in the legislation regarding the Stadium's redevelopment that only 24 'special events' (i.e. with attendance in excess of 25,000) a year can be hosted at the venue. This number of special events was reached when the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") faced the [Manly Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly_Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly Warringah Sea Eagles") in Round 26 of the 2011 NRL Telstra Premiership Season. On 6 September 2011, legislation was passed to lift the crowd capacity limit to 35,000 for those 24 events, enabling the Broncos to host finals matches should they progress that far.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The stadium's grass quality was criticised by coaches and players during the [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup").[[78]](#cite_note-78)
